sections.title: The N-Declension Pattern
sections.explanation: Certain masculine nouns in German add -(e)n in every case except the nominative singular. These are called 'weak nouns' (schwache Nomen). In the nominative singular they look normal, but in the accusative, dative, and genitive singular — and in all plural forms — they take the ending -(e)n. Compare: der Student (nominative) → den Studenten (accusative) → dem Studenten (dative) → des Studenten (genitive). This is different from regular masculine nouns like der Tisch, which only change the article: den Tisch, dem Tisch, des Tisches.
sections.tip: If the noun ends in -e, just add -n (der Junge → den Jungen). Otherwise add -en (der Student → den Studenten).

examples.de: Das Buch des Studenten ist neu.

exercises.prompt: Which is correct? 'I see the student.' → Ich sehe ___.
exercises.explanation: 'Student' is an n-declension noun. In the accusative it becomes 'den Studenten'.

exercises.prompt: Ich helfe dem ___. (I help the patient.) — use the dative of 'Patient'
exercises.hint: 'Patient' adds -en in all cases except nominative singular

exercises.prompt: Kennst du den ___? (Do you know the boy?) — use the accusative of 'Junge'
exercises.explanation: N-declension: der Junge → den Jungen. Nouns ending in -e add -n.

sections.title: Which Nouns Follow the N-Declension?
sections.explanation: N-declension nouns are almost always masculine and typically refer to living beings (people or animals). Common categories: 1) Masculine nouns ending in -e: der Junge (boy), der Kunde (customer), der Kollege (colleague), der Löwe (lion), der Affe (monkey). 2) Masculine nouns ending in -ent: der Student, der Patient, der Präsident. 3) Ending in -ant: der Elefant, der Demonstrant. 4) Ending in -ist: der Polizist, der Tourist, der Journalist. 5) Ending in -at: der Soldat, der Demokrat. 6) Ending in -oge: der Biologe, der Psychologe. 7) Other common ones: der Herr (gentleman/Mr.), der Mensch (person), der Nachbar (neighbor), der Bauer (farmer).
sections.tip: Most n-declension nouns are of foreign origin (Latin/Greek) and refer to people. If a masculine noun ends in -e, -ent, -ant, -ist, -at, or -oge, it almost certainly follows the n-declension.

examples.de: Das ist die Meinung des Menschen.

exercises.prompt: Which noun does NOT follow the n-declension?
exercises.explanation: 'Tisch' is a regular masculine noun (des Tisches, not des Tischen). 'Tourist' and 'Kollege' are n-declension nouns.

exercises.prompt: Match each nominative form with its accusative form:
exercises.explanation: All these are n-declension nouns. 'Herr' adds only -n; the others add -en or -n depending on whether they already end in -e.

exercises.prompt: Ich habe mit dem ___ gesprochen. (I spoke with the journalist.) — use the dative of 'Journalist'
exercises.hint: Nouns ending in -ist add -en in non-nominative cases

sections.title: Mixed Declension and Exceptions
sections.explanation: A small group of n-declension nouns also add -s in the genitive singular. These are called 'mixed declension' nouns. The most common are: der Name (name) → des Namens, der Gedanke (thought) → des Gedankens, der Glaube (belief) → des Glaubens, der Wille (will) → des Willens, der Friede (peace) → des Friedens, der Buchstabe (letter of alphabet) → des Buchstabens, das Herz (heart) → des Herzens. Note that 'das Herz' is the only neuter n-declension noun! In accusative and dative these use -n like normal n-declension: den Namen, dem Namen.
sections.tip: Mixed declension = n-declension + genitive -s. Remember: des Namens, des Herzens. These are exceptions you need to memorize.

examples.de: Wie ist der Name des Patienten?
Ich kenne den Namen des Mannes nicht.
examples.en: I don't know the man's name.

examples.de: Der Gedanke des Philosophen war neu.
Im Grunde seines Herzens weiß er es.
examples.en: In the bottom of his heart, he knows it.

exercises.prompt: What is the genitive of 'der Name'?
exercises.explanation: 'Name' is a mixed declension noun. It adds -n in accusative/dative (den Namen, dem Namen) but -ns in the genitive: des Namens.

exercises.prompt: Das ist die Bedeutung des ___. (That is the meaning of the thought.) — genitive of 'Gedanke'
exercises.hint: Mixed declension nouns add -ns in the genitive
exercises.explanation: Mixed declension: der Gedanke → des Gedankens (genitive with -ns).

exercises.prompt: Which is the only neuter noun that follows a form of n-declension?
exercises.explanation: 'Das Herz' is the only neuter n-declension noun: das Herz, das Herz (acc.), dem Herzen (dat.), des Herzens (gen.).
